translationEn: Truly Jua fulfilled her oath among a people whose brother's blood is not left unavenged. If Jua perishes, then every soul shall be driven back by those who drive them, as life's herders always do. If Jua perishes, then war — as you foresaw — shall be rekindled by those who light its flames. Your judgment did not fail you on the day of departure, when spears were leveled true by those who raised them for you. It was as though you knew, the day your garments were stripped, what fate awaited those who stripped them. Your vow shall be fulfilled — for vows always find their fulfilment when the shameful deed has been fully met. We struck the Khazraji clan with sharpened blades that wiped out the roots of their noble stock. The blood-price of the gazelles is not lacking in Ka'b's tribe, nor did fifty camels fall short for those who sought them. We did not say: a life for a life, demand from them recompense if they refuse blood-money. Rather, we sent that thirst away slaked, quenched by your memory by those who watered it. If the deeds of a living clan could reach the slain, then those who drew your swords would gladden you.
context: A poem of tribal pride and vengeance addressed to Jua, a woman who swore an oath of revenge. Ka'b praises his tribe's fulfilment of blood obligations and their martial resolve in avenging her.

titleEn: A Dishevelled Man Loose-Shouldered I Roused
translationEn: A dishevelled man with loose, drooping shoulders I roused from his rest, while the creep of sleep still lingered in his very bones.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I roused him, I dispatched him

context: A single-verse fragment describing the poet rousing a drowsy companion for a night errand or journey, vividly capturing the physical heaviness of interrupted sleep.

titleEn: For What Time Does a Man Hoard His Benefit

verses.sadr: إذا المرء لم ينفعك حيا فنفعه

translationEn: For what time does a man hoard his benefit, saying tomorrow and yet again tomorrow, while time itself comes and goes? When a man does not benefit you while he is alive, his benefit is little indeed once the tombstones are laid over him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: he hoards, he conceals, he saves up
time, fate, the passage of ages
coming in the morning and returning in the evening; ever-passing
flat stone slabs (used to cover graves)

context: A brief wisdom poem urging generosity in life, arguing that deferred giving is worthless, for once a man is buried under grave-stones his benefit to others ceases entirely.

titleEn: Know O Messenger of God You Shall Reach Me
translationEn: Know, O Messenger of God, that you shall surely overtake me, and that a threat from you is like being seized directly by the hand — certain and inescapable.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: like being taken by the hand; certain and direct

context: A single-verse acknowledgment by Ka'b ibn Zuhayr of the Prophet's authority, likely composed at the time of his famous ode Banat Su'ad, recognizing that the Prophet's reach is inescapable.

translationEn: The Prophet wiped his brow, and from that touch a brightness shines upon his cheeks. Upon his face is a silken radiance — the nobility of prophethood and of illustrious ancestors.
context: A short panegyric fragment praising the Prophet Muhammad's luminous appearance, likely commemorating the moment the Prophet touched the face of Ka'b or another companion, leaving a mark of blessed radiance.

titleAr: لاتفش سرك إلا عند ذي ثقة
titleEn: Reveal Not Your Secret Except to the Trustworthy

verses.sadr: لاتفش سرك إلا عند ذي ثقة

verses.ajuz: لم تخش منه لما استودعت إظهارا

translationEn: Do not reveal your secret except to one who is trustworthy — or else the best thing you can entrust is secrets kept locked away. A broad chest and a wide heart that remains silent — one from whom, when entrusted, you need not fear disclosure.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you reveal, you disclose, you spread
a person of trust, a reliable confidant
a broad chest; a capacious, trustworthy heart

context: A wisdom poem advising discretion and caution in entrusting secrets to others, counseling the listener to choose confidants wisely or to keep their counsel entirely to themselves.

titleAr: هل حبل رملة قبل البين مبتور
titleEn: Is Ramla's Rope Cut Before Departure

verses.sadr: هل حبل رملة قبل البين مبتور
verses.ajuz: أم انت بالحلم بعد الجهل معذور

verses.sadr: ما يجمع الشوق إن دار بنا شحطت
نشفى بها وهي داء لو تصاقبنا
ما روضة من رياض الحزن باكرها
ما أنس لا أنسها والدمع منسرب

verses.ajuz: أو مشعب من أتي البحر مفجور

verses.sadr: حتى إذا أبردوا قاموا إلى قلص
حتى سقى الليل سقي الجن فانغمست
verses.ajuz: في جوزه إذ دجا الآكام والقور

verses.sadr: ياخير من حملت نعلا له قدم
أعطال ربك فضلا لا زوال له
verses.ajuz: من أين أنى له الأيام تغيير

translationEn: Is Ramla's bond cut before the parting, or are you excused by patience after your folly? Longing gathers when distance separates us; and closeness leaves her just as forsaken. We seek cure in her, yet she herself is the disease — as the drunkard is cured by the very wine that ails him. No garden of the highlands visited at dawn, carpeted with varied-coloured rain-washed plants, is sweeter in its drifting fragrance on a day when perfumes are loved after sleep. I forget all things but not her — while tears stream down like pearls rolling along the cheek. When I saw them loading their camels I knew their claims were true, for parting is a dreaded thing. Their driver, a rough wary man, urges them on as though he bore a blood-grudge against all mankind. Their litters as they moved in retreat were like the palm trees of Nu'aynayn, clustered and heavy-laden — thick-necked palms watered by a flowing channel or a stream broken loose from the sea. Will Dha'laba, a gaunt she-camel whose saddle slips from her spine, carry me safely to the good? Behind her, young she-camels run with their reins loose, worn by night travel and the midday heat. They pound on with their riders, shedding the weariness of the sandal-strip, while the wild asses have already taken shelter in the shade from the sun. Until the chameleon straightened itself erect and the time came for the midday rest in the open desert. They said: move aside, then touched the ground and sought shade in a gap through which the dust gusts blow. They stayed as though a bird of prey perched over them, fluttering when the whirlwinds scattered around it — one side stripped bare by the wind's direction, the other held fast in the hands of the men. Until when the heat eased they rose and mounted she-camels like the bows of the bent shawhat wood — swift as a herd of wild asses startled in al-Si by a hunter who drove and scattered them. Until night watered them as jinn water, and they sank into its midst when the hills and highland rocks darkened. Night covered the lowland and the hills alike until both were submerged in the darkness of its blackness. Truly Ali is one whose leadership is blessed with good fortune, renowned for his righteous deeds. The son-in-law of the Prophet, the finest of men to boast of — for whoever contests his glory is himself surpassed. He prayed the ritual prayer with the unlettered Prophet before them all, before faith returned and the Lord of mankind was still denied. He stood firm against the tyrants of idolatry, striking them until they submitted and God's religion was victorious. You stood in justice as a trustee when the people of desire — those of passions and falsehood — turned against it. O best of those whose feet have ever borne a sandal after the Prophet — with him oppression is forsaken. May your Lord bestow upon you a grace that knows no ending — from whence can time bring about its change?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: cut, severed (of a rope or bond)
she became distant, she went far away
litters, caravans of women on camels
the chameleon (used as a sundial by Arabs)
swift she-camels moving with a rolling gait
one of blessed leadership, fortunate in his trust

context: A long ode that moves from a nasib (lyric prelude lamenting love and separation from Ramla), through a description of a desert journey, to a panegyric praising Ali ibn Abi Talib for his piety, justice, and proximity to the Prophet.

titleEn: A Night of Longing Whose Stars Seemed Scattered
translationEn: A night of longing — as though its stars had scattered and dispersed from it into green Persian cloaks, studding the darkness like scattered jewels.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one filled with longing, a yearner

context: A single-verse fragment describing a sleepless night of longing, in which the poet compares the stars scattered across the dark sky to jewels scattered over green Persian robes — a characteristically Pre-Islamic and early Islamic metaphor.

titleAr: كأن امرأ لم يلق عيشا بنعمة
titleEn: As Though a Man Had Never Enjoyed a Life of Ease

verses.sadr: كأن امرأ لم يلق عيشا بنعمة

translationEn: It is as though a man had never tasted a life of ease and well-being, once the back-breaking calamity descends upon him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: it is as though, as if
in ease, in comfort, in prosperity
a life, a manner of living

context: A single-verse maxim reflecting on how a great calamity makes all previous comfort feel as though it never existed — a theme of the transience of ease characteristic of early Arabic wisdom poetry.

titleEn: Silent and Eloquent His Silence Is of Wisdom

verses.sadr: فتى لم يدع رشدا ولم يأت منكرا
verses.ajuz: ولم يدر من فضل السماحة ما البخل

verses.sadr: إذا كان نجل الفحل بين نجيبة

translationEn: He is silent and yet eloquent — his silence is born of forbearance, and his decisive speech dispels doubt through knowledge. A young man who has never abandoned right guidance nor committed wrong, and who — through the excellence of his generosity — has never even come to know what miserliness is. Through him a radiant sun gave birth to a full moon, a blessed sun by which both branch and root grow tall. When the offspring of a noble stallion is born between a thoroughbred mare and a generous pure-bred dam, the offspring is ennobled.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who is habitually silent, reticent
his decisive speech, his clear and judicious words
a noble thoroughbred she-camel; a woman of noble lineage
a pure white, pure-bred she-camel; a woman of pure lineage

context: A panegyric in praise of a noble young man, celebrating his combination of eloquent silence and decisive speech, his moral uprightness, and his noble lineage on both his father's and mother's sides.

titleEn: There Is No Hope for One Who Rides Peril

verses.sadr: إذا أنت لم تقصر عن الجهل والخنا

translationEn: There is no hope for one who rushes headlong into danger, and no one can bear the burden that God has cast down. If you do not hold back from ignorance and indecency, you will either encounter a forbearing man — or a fool will encounter you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a desired aim, hope, a sought-after goal
a man's load, his burden; his entire belongings
you hold back from, you refrain from

context: A wisdom fragment urging restraint from recklessness and indecency, warning that rash behavior will always bring consequences — whether the exposure of one's own ignorance or the retribution of a wiser adversary.

titleEn: Do You Expect My Apology O Son of Arwa

verses.ajuz: عن الحق قدما غال حلمك غول

translationEn: Do you expect my apology, O son of Arwa, when you have long turned me away from the truth? A demon has devoured your forbearance. My imprecation upon you for what you have wrought shall last every day and night, and it shall be long. And my exile through the lands, my estrangement, and my being abused for the sake of God — all of that is but little.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you turned me back, you deflected me
a demon has consumed your forbearance; your patience has been devoured
my imprecation, my prayer against someone
my estrangement, my being a stranger far from home
for the sake of God, in God's cause

context: A poem of reproach and pride directed at an adversary identified as Ibn Arwa, in which Ka'b refuses to apologize, declares his steadfastness in God's cause, and frames his own suffering of exile and insult as a minor sacrifice.

titleEn: The Hunters Surrounded Their Prey and Behold
translationEn: The hunters encircled their prey that startled them — and behold, some of the hunters were themselves slain by the arrows of the game.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the arrows of the game (metaphorically, the prey turns hunter)

context: A single-verse proverb-like image depicting the reversal of hunter and hunted — a wisdom motif common in early Arabic poetry warning that the pursuer may himself become the victim.

titleAr: له عنق تلوي بما وصلت به
titleEn: She Has a Neck That Twists with Its Ornaments

verses.sadr: له عنق تلوي بما وصلت به

translationEn: She has a neck that twists gracefully with whatever adorns it, and two sides that outpace every riding litter in the caravan.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: what was connected to it, what adorns it (jewelry or ornaments)
two sides, two flanks (of a she-camel)
they outpace, they surpass, they overtake
riding litters, caravans of women (singular: ظعينة)

context: A single-verse descriptive fragment praising a swift she-camel, combining the elegance of her neck's movement with the speed of her flanks as they outpace the other caravan litters.

titleEn: By Your Life I Did Not Fear for My Father
translationEn: By your life, it was not the battlefields between Qaww and al-Sali that I feared for my father. Rather what I feared for my father was the consequence of his spear among all the tribes — the consequence of a young man whose bonds are loosened, whose cord is tightly twisted, who leads both to right guidance and to peril. Oh, the grief of the widows and the orphans, and the grief of the weeping women over my father.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: by your life (a common oath formula)
battlefields, places of wrestling down and slaying
the consequence of a deed; a crime, a guilt incurred
one whose bonds are loosened yet whose cord is tightly twisted; energetic and unrestrained
one who commands, who leads, who directs

context: A lament for Ka'b's father Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma, expressing that the poet's fear was never for his father's safety in battle but for the turbulent consequences of his father's deeds and influence — closing with an elegy for the widows and orphans his father leaves behind.
